title: Socrates
summary: An Athenian philosopher who wrote nothing but transformed Western philosophy — through the practice of public questioning he called elenchus, his conviction that the unexamined life is not worth living, and his trial and execution for impiety and corrupting the youth of Athens, which became the defining image of philosophy as a dangerous practice.
hook: Socrates is the most influential philosopher in Western history who left no writings. Everything we know about him comes from others — primarily his student Plato, who may have used Socrates as a mask for his own ideas. The 'Socratic problem' — the question of what the historical Socrates actually believed — has never been fully resolved. What is certain is that his method of questioning, his insistence that wisdom begins with the acknowledgment of ignorance, and his willingness to die for his philosophical commitments transformed what philosophy was understood to be.

beginner.theFigure: Socrates was an Athenian philosopher born around 470 BCE. He died in 399 BCE, condemned to death by an Athenian jury. He was the son of a stonemason and a midwife. He served as a soldier and was known for his physical endurance. He was famously ugly. He spent his adult life in the Agora — the public square of Athens — talking with anyone willing to engage him in philosophical conversation. He charged no fees. He left no writings. Everything we know about him comes from others.
beginner.worldOfIdeas: Athens in Socrates's time was full of competing claims about wisdom and knowledge. The sophists were professional teachers who claimed to teach civic virtue and argumentation for fees. They were interested in persuasion rather than truth. Socrates positioned himself as the opposite. He claimed to know nothing. He charged nothing. He was interested not in winning arguments but in finding out what was actually true. His starting point was strange: an oracle had declared him the wisest man in Athens. He found this baffling, since he knew himself to be ignorant.
beginner.centralIdea: Socrates's central practice was the elenchus — a method of questioning. He would ask someone to define a concept they clearly valued: justice, piety, courage, or beauty. Then, through careful questions, he would show that their definition led to contradictions. The goal was not to humiliate but to produce aporia — a state of productive puzzlement. The interlocutor would recognise that they didn't know something they thought they knew. Socrates believed this recognition was the beginning of wisdom. His most famous claim: the unexamined life is not worth living.
beginner.howSpread: Socrates's ideas survived his death through his students. The most important was Plato. Plato wrote a series of philosophical dialogues in which Socrates is almost always the main speaker. Through Plato's dialogues, Socrates became the ancestor of virtually all subsequent Western philosophy. Aristotle, who studied at Plato's Academy, carried the tradition forward. The Roman philosopher Cicero said that Socrates brought philosophy down from the heavens and into the city — turning it from abstract speculation about nature toward the question of how human beings should live.
beginner.contestedInheritance: Socrates's legacy is contested in two directions. From one direction, he is the father of rational inquiry — the man who insisted that beliefs must be tested by argument. From another, he is a critic of democracy. His trial was conducted by a democratic Athenian jury. His most famous students included Alcibiades — who betrayed Athens — and Critias — who led a brutal oligarchic regime. Karl Popper argued that Plato's Socrates was an enemy of the open society. Others argue his willingness to accept his jury's verdict — even a wrong one — makes him a democratic hero.

general.theFigure: The 'Socratic problem' — the question of what the historical Socrates actually believed, as distinct from Plato's philosophical views expressed through a Socratic mask — is one of the most persistent puzzles in the history of philosophy. The sources agree on certain biographical facts: Socrates was Athenian, born around 470 BCE, served as a hoplite in the Peloponnesian War, engaged in philosophical conversation in public spaces, and was tried and executed in 399 BCE on charges of impiety and corrupting the youth. They disagree, sometimes radically, about his philosophical positions. Plato's Socrates is a metaphysician who believes in the immortality of the soul and the existence of abstract Forms. Xenophon's Socrates is a practical moralist with conventional religious views. Aristophanes's Socrates, in The Clouds, is a sophist and natural philosopher — interested precisely in the cosmological questions the historical Socrates supposedly rejected. The triangulation of these sources has been a major preoccupation of Socratic scholarship.
general.worldOfIdeas: The intellectual context of Socratic Athens was shaped by three competing traditions. The pre-Socratic natural philosophers — Thales, Anaximander, Heraclitus, Parmenides, and others — had developed accounts of the natural world based on reason rather than mythology; Socrates deliberately turned away from these cosmological questions. The sophists — Protagoras, Gorgias, Thrasymachus — were developing the arts of rhetoric and relativism, arguing that justice was what the strong defined it as and that persuasion was the highest human skill. And the emerging civic culture of democratic Athens was producing its own implicit philosophy — the idea that the assembled citizens were collectively wise and that political judgment was a matter of majority decision rather than expert knowledge. Socrates's philosophy was a response to all three: rejecting cosmological speculation as a distraction from the question of how to live, rejecting sophistic relativism as incompatible with the possibility of truth, and implicitly challenging democratic majoritarianism by insisting that virtue required knowledge rather than consensus.
general.centralIdea: The elenchus — Socratic cross-examination — operated through a characteristic sequence. Socrates would identify an interlocutor who claimed to know something (what piety is, what courage is, what justice is). He would ask for a definition. The interlocutor would provide one. Socrates would then find a counterexample — a case that the definition covered but that the interlocutor would not want to call pious, or courageous, or just. The interlocutor would revise their definition to exclude the counterexample. Socrates would find another counterexample. The process would continue until the interlocutor either gave up in frustration or arrived at genuine aporia — the recognition that they did not, after all, know what they thought they knew. The philosophical significance of this procedure is debated: is the elenchus a method for arriving at truth, or only for clearing away false beliefs? The early dialogues almost never reach positive conclusions; they end in aporia. This has led some scholars (Gregory Vlastos most influentially) to argue that the elenchus is itself Socrates's positive contribution — that the recognition of ignorance is philosophically productive in its own right.
general.howSpread: The transmission of Socratic philosophy to subsequent history operated through several channels. The most important was Plato's Academy, founded around 387 BCE, which became the model for all subsequent philosophical schools. Plato's dialogues were the primary curriculum of the Academy and have been continuously read, in some tradition or another, since then. The Stoics and Epicureans, who dominated Hellenistic philosophy, both claimed Socratic inspiration — the Stoics for Socrates's indifference to physical comfort and his insistence that virtue was sufficient for happiness, the Epicureans for his critique of conventional goods. In the Roman world, Cicero's philosophical works transmitted Socratic ideas in Latin. The early Christian tradition found in Socrates a pagan prefiguration of philosophical martyrdom. In the Islamic philosophical tradition, Socrates (as Suqrat) was known through Arabic translations of Greek texts. The Renaissance recovery of Plato's complete dialogues made Socrates newly available to European philosophy.
general.contestedInheritance: The most important modern debate about Socrates concerns the relationship between his philosophy and Athenian democracy. The trial of 399 BCE took place five years after the restoration of democracy following the rule of the Thirty Tyrants — a brutal oligarchic regime that had included several of Socrates's associates. The charges against him — impiety and corrupting the youth — may have been, in part, a retrospective punishment for his association with Athens's enemies. But there is also a genuine philosophical tension: Socrates's insistence that virtue required knowledge, that political wisdom was not a natural endowment of citizens but a rare achievement requiring philosophical training, sits uneasily with democratic principles of equal political standing. I.F. Stone's The Trial of Socrates (1988) argued, from a democratic perspective, that Athens had legitimate reasons to be suspicious of Socrates. Most scholars find this argument overstated but take seriously the political dimensions of the trial.

advanced.theFigure: The most methodologically sophisticated approach to the Socratic problem treats the different ancient sources as providing partial and perspectival views of a historical figure that cannot be recovered whole. Gregory Vlastos's Socrates: Ironist and Moral Philosopher (1991) distinguishes between 'Socrates-E' (the Socrates of the early, elenctic dialogues) and 'Socrates-M' (the Socrates of the middle dialogues, who holds the metaphysical doctrines of Forms and recollection). Vlastos argues that only Socrates-E represents the historical Socrates; the metaphysical doctrines are Plato's own innovations. This thesis has been influential but contested: Charles Kahn and others argue for a more unified reading of the dialogues in which Platonic metaphysics is already implicit in the early works.
advanced.worldOfIdeas: The relationship between Socratic philosophy and the sophistic tradition is more complex than the standard opposition suggests. Both Socrates and the sophists were responding to the same cultural crisis: the collapse of traditional mythological authority and the need for a new basis for ethics and political life. The sophists' answer was pragmatic and relativistic: there is no truth, only persuasion, and the good life consists in pursuing power and pleasure effectively. Socrates's answer was that the collapse of tradition opened the possibility of genuine philosophical inquiry into what was actually true. But recent scholarship — particularly Paul Woodruff's work on Socratic ignorance — has emphasized that Socrates shared more with the sophists than the tradition acknowledges: his claim to ignorance was itself a sophisticated rhetorical performance, and his elenchus was a form of competitive display that drew on the same agonistic culture that produced sophistic rhetoric.
advanced.centralIdea: The question of whether Socrates had positive doctrines — positions he actually believed, as distinct from arguments he used to expose others' ignorance — has divided scholarship for a century. Vlastos's influential answer was that Socrates did have positive moral beliefs (that virtue is knowledge, that wrongdoing is always a product of ignorance, that it is worse to do injustice than to suffer it) and that the elenchus was his method for testing and refining them. Myles Burnyeat's challenge to this view — arguing that Socratic disavowal of knowledge is sincere and that the elenchus does not yield positive results — has produced a continuing debate about Socratic epistemology. The question is not merely exegetical: it bears on what philosophy is for, whether it can produce knowledge or only clear away error.
advanced.howSpread: The reception history of Socrates in the Islamic philosophical tradition deserves particular attention as an example of cross-cultural transmission. Socrates was known to Islamic philosophers primarily through the doxographical tradition — summaries of philosophical positions rather than complete texts — and through Arabic translations of some Platonic dialogues. Al-Farabi, Ibn Rushd (Averroes), and others engaged with Socratic ideas within frameworks shaped by Islamic theology and Aristotelian metaphysics. The figure of Socrates in this tradition was often assimilated to the ideal of the prophet or the wise man (hakim) — a figure whose wisdom derived from divine illumination rather than dialectical inquiry alone. This assimilation produced a distinctively different Socrates from the one familiar to modern Western philosophy.
advanced.contestedInheritance: The reception of the trial of Socrates as a political event has been shaped by the specific political contexts of its interpreters. In the 18th century Enlightenment, Socrates became a martyr to free thought persecuted by religious authority — a proto-philosophe executed by the Athenian equivalent of the Church. In the 19th century, Hegel's reading in the Lectures on the History of Philosophy treated the trial as a genuine tragedy: Socrates represented the emerging principle of individual moral autonomy, Athens represented the still-valid principle of communal ethical life (Sittlichkeit), and the clash between them was irresolvable — both sides were right. In the 20th century, the trial became a test case for debates about liberal democracy and its limits: I.F. Stone's defense of the Athenian jury and Karl Popper's condemnation of Platonic politics as proto-totalitarian represent the extremes of a debate that has not been resolved.

foundation.theStory: Socrates was an Athenian philosopher who wrote nothing but transformed Western philosophy through his practice of public questioning. He was tried and executed in 399 BCE for impiety and corrupting the youth of Athens. His method — the elenchus, or cross-examination of assumptions — and his insistence that wisdom begins with the recognition of ignorance became the foundation of the Western philosophical tradition.
foundation.theDebate: The central philosophical debate about Socrates concerns what he actually believed — the 'Socratic problem.' Since he wrote nothing, all we know comes from others, especially Plato, who may have used Socrates as a mouthpiece for his own ideas. The central political debate concerns his relationship to democracy: was his trial a legitimate response to a philosopher whose teachings had undermined Athenian democracy, or a travesty of justice that proved democracy's capacity for self-betrayal?
foundation.whyItMatters: Socrates matters because he established what philosophy is: not the accumulation of knowledge but the rigorous questioning of assumptions, especially about how to live. His method of asking basic questions about justice, courage, and piety — and refusing to accept comfortable answers — is the model for all subsequent critical inquiry. His trial and death established the image of the philosopher as someone whose commitment to truth can put them in conflict with political authority.
questions: Socrates claimed that 'the unexamined life is not worth living.' What does this mean? Do you find it convincing? Consider the objection that most people live meaningful lives without philosophical examination, and that the claim is elitist. How would Socrates respond to this objection, and how would you evaluate his response?

At his trial, Socrates was offered the chance to propose his own punishment — probably exile — but instead proposed that he be given free meals at public expense. The jury, angered, voted for death. Was Socrates being courageous, foolish, or deliberately seeking martyrdom? What does his choice tell us about his philosophical commitments?

interpretation.scholarlyConversation: The dominant framework for Socratic scholarship in the 20th century was established by Gregory Vlastos, whose Socrates: Ironist and Moral Philosopher (1991) distinguished sharply between the historical Socrates (reconstructed from the early dialogues) and the Platonic Socrates (the mouthpiece of the middle and late dialogues). Vlastos argued that the historical Socrates had genuine positive moral beliefs — that virtue is knowledge, that wrongdoing is always a product of ignorance — even while denying that he had knowledge in the philosophers' sense. This interpretation has been challenged by scholars who find Vlastos's distinction between early and late dialogues too sharp and by those who question whether Socratic disavowal of knowledge was ever sincere.
interpretation.evidenceAndLimits: The evidentiary constraints on Socratic scholarship are severe. Plato's dialogues are the primary source, but Plato was a creative philosopher in his own right who developed increasingly ambitious philosophical doctrines over a long career. Xenophon's Socrates is more conventional and less philosophically interesting; scholars debate whether this reflects the historical Socrates or Xenophon's limitations as a philosopher. Aristophanes's The Clouds presents a satirical Socrates, but satire requires some connection to recognizable reality. The triangulation of these sources has produced a sophisticated scholarly conversation that cannot reach certainty.
interpretation.takingAPosition: The most defensible position on Socrates distinguishes between what can and cannot be known. What can be known: Socrates practiced the elenchus, claimed ignorance, charged no fees, engaged in public philosophical conversation, was associated with politically problematic figures, and was convicted and executed in 399 BCE. What cannot be known with confidence: the specific philosophical doctrines attributed to him in the middle Platonic dialogues — the theory of Forms, the doctrine of recollection — which are more plausibly Platonic innovations. The 'Socratic irony' — the question of whether his claims to ignorance were sincere or performances — remains genuinely unresolvable.
questions: The sophists argued that justice is whatever the powerful define it as, and that morality is a social construction designed to restrain the strong for the benefit of the weak. Socrates argued that virtue is a form of knowledge that exists independently of social convention. Which position do you find more defensible, and why? What evidence would you need to decisively resolve this dispute?

Plato's Socrates says in the Apology that he would disobey an Athenian order to stop philosophizing because his duty to god (or to philosophical inquiry) supersedes his duty to the city. But in the Crito, the same Socrates refuses to escape prison because he owes obedience to the laws of Athens. Are these positions consistent? What theory of the relationship between individual conscience and political authority do they imply?

research.topic: The Socratic problem: reconstructing the historical Socrates
research.content: The question of what the historical Socrates believed, as distinct from Plato's philosophical views, has been the central preoccupation of Socratic scholarship for over a century. Gregory Vlastos's distinction between early and late Platonic dialogues as evidence for the historical Socrates versus Plato's own views has been enormously influential, though contested. The consensus that the Apology, Euthyphro, Meno, and other early dialogues are more historically reliable than the Republic or Phaedo is widely accepted but not universally so. Charles Kahn's Plato and the Socratic Dialogue (1996) challenges the developmentalist reading entirely.

research.topic: The political dimensions of the trial of 399 BCE
research.content: The trial of Socrates has been analyzed extensively as a political event — specifically as a response to his association with Alcibiades and Critias, two figures who had caused catastrophic damage to Athenian democracy. Thomas Brickhouse and Nicholas Smith's Socrates on Trial (1989) provides the most comprehensive analysis of the charges and the trial's context. I.F. Stone's The Trial of Socrates (1988) argued provocatively that Athens had legitimate reasons for the prosecution; most scholars find this argument overstated but take seriously the political dimensions of a trial that occurred five years after the restoration of democracy.

research.topic: Socrates's own philosophical views in his own voice
research.content: Socrates wrote nothing. The philosophical positions attributed to him come entirely from others who had their own philosophical agendas. What would have been needed to recover his actual views: a philosophical autobiography, notes, letters, or any document in his own hand. Why unavailable: deliberate choice — Socrates apparently believed that writing fixed thought and prevented the living dialogue through which genuine understanding is achieved. What can be responsibly inferred: the practices attributed to him (the elenchus, the disavowal of knowledge, the focus on ethical questions) are consistent across multiple independent sources and can be treated as historically reliable. What must remain unknowable: whether the specific doctrines in the early Platonic dialogues represent Socratic or Platonic positions, and what Socrates actually believed about the soul, the afterlife, and the nature of the good.

comparativeNarrative.name: The Founder of Ethics
comparativeNarrative.content: The dominant Western philosophical tradition treats Socrates as the founder of ethics as a philosophical discipline — the man who turned philosophy from cosmological speculation about the nature of the universe toward the question of how human beings should live. Aristotle credited Socrates with introducing inductive arguments and universal definitions into philosophical discussion. Cicero's formulation — that Socrates brought philosophy down from the heavens and into the city — established the image of Socrates as the philosopher of human life. This image was enormously productive: it made every subsequent philosopher's engagement with ethics a continuation of a Socratic conversation.

comparativeNarrative.name: The Dangerous Teacher
comparativeNarrative.content: From within the democratic tradition, Socrates is a more ambiguous figure than the standard philosophical hagiography suggests. His most famous students included Alcibiades, who persuaded Athens to the disastrous Sicilian Expedition and then defected to Sparta, and Critias, who led the murderous oligarchy of the Thirty Tyrants. His method of questioning democratic assumptions — asking whether the assembled citizens really knew what was good, whether majority vote was a reliable guide to truth — was a genuine challenge to democratic legitimacy. I.F. Stone's The Trial of Socrates presented a democratic defense of the verdict, arguing that Socrates's anti-democratic tendencies were real and that Athens had legitimate political reasons for its decision.

comparativeNarrative.name: The Pagan Saint
comparativeNarrative.content: Early Christian writers found in Socrates a pagan prefiguration of Christian martyrdom — a man who died for truth, who spoke of the soul's immortality, and who was persecuted by the established order. Justin Martyr in the 2nd century CE described Socrates as a proto-Christian, someone who had been guided by the same divine logos that was fully revealed in Christ. This assimilation of Socrates to Christian narrative was enormously influential in making Greek philosophy acceptable to Christian theology, but it also substantially distorted both: it made Socratic uncertainty look more like theological faith, and it made Socratic moral argument look more like divine revelation than it actually was.

rabbitHole.label: Plato
rabbitHole.reason: Socrates's most important student and the primary source for his philosophy — without Plato's dialogues, Socrates would be as obscure as most of the pre-Socratics

rabbitHole.label: The Republic
rabbitHole.reason: Plato's dialogue in which the Socratic method of questioning reaches its fullest expression — and where the question of what justice is receives its most systematic treatment

rabbitHole.label: Aristotle
rabbitHole.reason: Plato's student and Socrates's philosophical grandson — who carried forward the tradition while systematically revising it

rabbitHole.label: Athens
rabbitHole.reason: The political and cultural world that produced Socrates — and that ultimately put him on trial

rabbitHole.label: Democracy
rabbitHole.reason: The Athenian democratic system that tried and executed Socrates — and whose relationship to philosophical authority Socrates's life and death permanently problematized

rabbitHole.label: The Sophists
rabbitHole.reason: Protagoras, Gorgias, and the professional teachers of rhetoric whose relativism Socrates opposed — and without whom his own philosophical project cannot be understood

rabbitHole.label: Stoicism
rabbitHole.reason: The philosophical school that most directly claimed Socratic descent — taking from Socrates the ideas that virtue is sufficient for happiness and that the philosopher should be indifferent to external goods

reference.author: Plato
reference.title: The Last Days of Socrates (Euthyphro, Apology, Crito, Phaedo)
reference.annotation: The primary source for Socrates's philosophy and death — written by his student Plato in ancient Greek. The Apology is the most historically reliable account of Socrates's own self-presentation; the Phaedo dramatizes his final hours. Readers should be aware that even in these early dialogues, Plato's own philosophical views may be shaping the portrait.

reference.author: Xenophon
reference.title: Memorabilia (Apomnemoneumata)
reference.annotation: Xenophon's recollections of Socrates offer a very different portrait from Plato's — more practical, less metaphysical, more conventionally religious. Written in ancient Greek, the Memorabilia is essential for triangulating the historical Socrates against Plato's account.

reference.author: Gregory Vlastos
reference.title: Socrates: Ironist and Moral Philosopher
reference.annotation: The most influential 20th-century scholarly account of Socrates — Vlastos's distinction between the historical Socrates of the early dialogues and the Platonic Socrates of the later ones has shaped all subsequent scholarship, even for those who disagree.

reference.author: Robin Waterfield
reference.title: Why Socrates Died: Dispelling the Myths
reference.annotation: The clearest account of the political dimensions of the trial, situating it within Athens after the Peloponnesian War. Essential for understanding why Socrates was prosecuted and what the charges actually meant in their political context.

reference.author: I.F. Stone
reference.title: The Trial of Socrates
reference.annotation: A provocative democratic defense of the Athenian jury, arguing that Athens had legitimate political reasons for the prosecution. Most scholars find Stone's argument overstated, but its challenge to philosophical hagiography of Socrates is genuinely productive.

commerce.title: Plato: Five Dialogues (Euthyphro, Apology, Crito, Meno, Phaedo)
commerce.author: Plato (trans. G.M.A. Grube)
commerce.note: Start with the Apology — Socrates's own defense at his trial — which is the most direct window into his philosophical self-presentation

commerce.title: Socrates: A Very Short Introduction
commerce.author: C.C.W. Taylor
commerce.note: The clearest short overview of the Socratic problem and the main philosophical issues — ideal before or after reading the dialogues themselves

commerce.title: Why Socrates Died
commerce.author: Robin Waterfield
commerce.note: The best account of the trial's political context — essential for understanding why Athens executed its most famous philosopher

commerce.title: Socrates — Roberto Rossellini (1971)
commerce.note: Rossellini's television film is unusually faithful to the philosophical and historical sources — rare serious cinematic engagement with ancient philosophy

commerce.title: The Trial of Socrates
commerce.author: I.F. Stone
commerce.note: Start here if you want to understand the political context of Socrates' trial — Stone argues that Athens had genuine reasons to prosecute Socrates and that the standard philosophical account is too sympathetic. A productive provocation.

commerce.title: The Apology, Crito, and Phaedo
commerce.author: Plato
commerce.note: The essential primary sources — Plato's account of Socrates' trial, his refusal to escape, and his final conversation are the foundation of all subsequent engagement with Socrates. Short enough to read carefully in a single sitting.

commerce.title: Socrates: Ironist and Moral Philosopher
commerce.author: Gregory Vlastos
commerce.note: The essential scholarly account — Vlastos's careful analytical reading distinguishes between the historical Socrates and Plato's philosophical construction, and identifies the Socratic method's distinctive character.

popularCulture.title: The Death of Socrates — Jacques-Louis David (1787)
popularCulture.description: David's painting shows Socrates reaching calmly for the hemlock cup while his students grieve around him. It became one of the defining images of the Enlightenment's self-understanding — Socrates as a martyr to reason persecuted by ignorance and authority. The painting is historically inaccurate (it looks Roman, not Greek) but its cultural power in shaping how the 18th century understood the relationship between philosophy and political authority was immense.

popularCulture.title: The Clouds — Aristophanes (423 BCE)
popularCulture.description: Aristophanes's comedy portrays Socrates as a charlatan running a 'Think-Shop' where he teaches young men to argue their way out of paying debts. The portrayal is unfair — but it was written while Socrates was still alive and tells us something real about how ordinary Athenians viewed the philosophical movement. Socrates himself mentions this play at his trial as having damaged his reputation.

popularCulture.title: The Death of Socrates — Fictional Reconstructions
popularCulture.description: Socrates's final hours have been reimagined in countless novels, plays, and films across the centuries. The most notable is the Phaedo itself — Plato's account of Socrates spending his last hours discussing the immortality of the soul, then dying calmly after drinking hemlock. Whatever its historical accuracy, it is one of the most powerful death scenes in world literature.

popularCulture.title: The Socratic Method in Education
popularCulture.description: The term 'Socratic method' now describes a teaching technique — drawing out understanding through questions rather than lecturing — used in law schools, medical schools, and progressive classrooms worldwide. His core insight — that understanding must be actively produced, not passively received — remains one of the most influential ideas in the history of education.

popularCulture.title: The Socratic method in education and law
popularCulture.description: The Socratic method — eliciting contradictions through systematic questioning rather than lecturing — has become the dominant pedagogy of American legal education. What this reveals: that Socrates' practice of questioning assumptions rather than transmitting knowledge has been institutionalized in ways that preserve its form while transforming its purpose — from the pursuit of self-knowledge to the training of professional arguers.
